results.possibleIdentity: Charles Schwartz (1922–1998) was a prominent New York-based composer, author, and music impresario active from the late 1950s through the 1990s. He is best known for spearheading the "Composers' Showcase," a landmark concert series that began in 1957 and found a permanent home at the Whitney Museum of American Art in 1968. The series was a major venue for avant-garde, jazz, and contemporary classical music, featuring collaborations and performances by iconic figures such as John Cage, Merce Cunningham, David Tudor, Ornette Coleman, and Charles Mingus. Schwartz's association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is evidenced by his frequent moderation of public discussions and events involving E.A.T. founders and key figures like John Cage and David Tudor. He was a resident of Westbeth Artists Housing, a community known for its concentration of experimental and E.A.T.-affiliated artists. Beyond his work as a producer, Schwartz was a noted biographer of George Gershwin (1973) and Cole Porter (1977), and a composer of "jazz symphonies" like "Professor Jive." In 1972, he also co-founded "Let There Be Neon" in SoHo with artist Rudi Stern, a gallery and studio that explored neon as a medium for both art and commercial design.

Charles Schwartz was a physicist at the University of California, Berkeley, during the late 1960s and 1970s. While primarily a scientist, he was a radical activist and a founding member of Scientists and Engineers for Social and Political Action (SESPA), also known as "Science for the People." He was deeply involved in the era's movement to bridge science with social and political action, often opposing military-funded research on campuses. His profile fits the scientist/engineer side of the collaborations promoted by the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, which actively sought to pair artists with researchers and engineers to explore the social and creative implications of new technology.

results.possibleIdentity: Helen Goldberg (also identified as Helen G. Goldberg) was an artist-craftsman active in New York during the late 1960s and 1970s. She was a member of the Artist-Craftsmen of New York, Inc., and participated in their annual exhibitions, including the third annual exhibition in 1970 where she was listed as a participant from Hewlett Harbor, N.Y. Her work in this period focused on textile arts, specifically tie-dye and fiber techniques. In August 1970, she was a participant in the Fourth Biennial Meeting of the World Crafts Council in Dublin, Ireland, where she was documented discussing tie-dye techniques with international artists. Her artistic practice expanded into hand papermaking, and her work was later included in the Hand Papermaking Artist Registry. She is also recorded as a donor of artwork (such as an Alison Saar woodcut) to the Hofstra University Museum of Art.

Helen Goldberg (born 1930) was the second wife of the influential contemporary art curator and museum director Walter Hopps. They were married in 1970 and divorced in 1977. Hopps was a seminal figure in the post-war art world, co-founding the Ferus Gallery and serving as the director of the Corcoran Gallery of Art and the Menil Collection. He was a close collaborator and personal friend of artist Robert Rauschenberg, a co-founder of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Given her marriage to Hopps during the peak years of his involvement with Rauschenberg and the contemporary avant-garde scene in Washington D.C. and New York, she was deeply integrated into the artistic circle associated with E.A.T. during the late 1960s and early 1970s.

results.possibleIdentity: Ellsworth Snyder (1931–2005), who often styled his name in lowercase as 'ellsworth snyder,' was an American abstract painter, professional pianist, conductor, and scholar. He was active in the late 1960s and 1970s as a champion of avant-garde art and music. Snyder was a close associate and lifelong friend of composer John Cage, who was a key figure in the founding of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Snyder's association with E.A.T. is documented through collaborative publications and archives, including a 1992 publication titled 'E.A.T. (Experiments in Art and Technology), John Cage & Ellsworth Snyder, Two Texts Concerning John Cage; Chronology and Autobiography.' He wrote the first doctoral dissertation on John Cage (1970) and was involved in the Fluxus movement, curated Fluxus archives, and performed unconventional new music lectures during the late 1960s. Although often cited as Ellsworth J. Snyder, his prominent role in the E.A.T.-related avant-garde scene of that era makes him the most likely artist match.

E. C. Snyder, Jr. was an engineer at Bell Telephone Laboratories (Bell Labs) during the early 1960s and 1970s. Bell Labs was the primary technical partner for Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), providing the engineers who collaborated with artists like Robert Rauschenberg and John Cage. Snyder is documented in the 'Bell System Technical Journal' (1963) for his role in coordinating technical systems for the Telstar satellite project, specifically the autotrack system at Andover, Maine. He later contributed to research on DC-DC converters (1977). In the context of E.A.T., which formalised the 'Artist-Engineer' collaboration model, engineers were frequently credited alongside artists in project directories and catalogs, such as the membership lists for 'Some More Beginnings' (1968) and the 'Pepsi Pavilion' (1970).

results.possibleIdentity: Jane Weissman is an American artist, writer, and historian who has been active in the New York City art scene since the late 1960s. She is best known for her documentation and leadership within the community mural movement, which began in New York around 1968. Weissman is the co-author of the seminal book "On the Wall: Four Decades of Community Murals in New York City" (2009) and served as the longtime administrative director of Artmakers Inc., a community mural collective founded in 1985. She has curated numerous exhibitions, including "Images of the African Diaspora in New York City Community Murals" and "La Lucha Continua The Struggle Continues: 1985 & 2017." Her work often focuses on the social, political, and cultural context of public art collaborations between artists and neighborhood residents, a theme that mirrors the collaborative goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

Julian Weissman was a New York-based art writer, critic, and gallery director active during the 1970s. He frequently published reviews and articles in major art journals under the name J. Weissman, including a notable piece on the photographer Eugène Atget for "Art News" in January 1976. Julian Weissman was deeply embedded in the postwar New York art world, maintaining professional and personal relationships with prominent artists such as Elaine and Willem de Kooning, Sol LeWitt, and Grace Hartigan. He served as a director for galleries like the Gruenebaum Gallery and later founded Julian Weissman Fine Art. His activities during the late 1960s and early 1970s included managing artist relationships and participating in the critical discourse surrounding contemporary art and photography.

Ken Weissman (Kenneth Weissman) is a film and video editor who is historically associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). He is a primary member of the preservation team that restored and edited the archival film and audio recordings of E.A.T.'s foundational 1966 series "9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering." Working alongside E.A.T. founders Billy Klüver and Julie Martin, Weissman was responsible for completing the performance sections of films documenting artists like Robert Rauschenberg, John Cage, and Öyvind Fahlström. While his most visible work on these projects took place during the 1990s and 2000s to create a permanent historical record, his name is often cited in the context of E.A.T.'s primary collaborative projects from the late 1960s.
